content.text: What is the typical half-life of caffeine in adults, and what factors cause it to vary?

The mean elimination half-life of caffeine in healthy adults is around 5 hours, though it ranges widely from 1.5 to 9.5 hours according to studies in the Annual Review of Nutrition. Several factors cause variation. Smoking roughly halves the half-life because nicotine induces the CYP1A2 enzyme responsible for caffeine metabolism, according to research published in Clinical Pharmacokinetics. Pregnancy extends it significantly, with the third trimester showing half-lives of 10-15 hours. Oral contraceptives can double the half-life. Genetic variation in the CYP1A2 gene produces fast and slow metabolizers, and this single polymorphism explains a large fraction of inter-individual variability per a study in the European Journal of Clinical Pharmacology.
Does caffeine affect sleep quality even when consumed many hours before bed?

Yes. A controlled study published in the Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine found that 400 mg of caffeine consumed 6 hours before bedtime reduced total sleep time by more than 1 hour compared to placebo. Sleep efficiency also dropped measurably. The authors concluded that even afternoon caffeine can impair sleep, and the effect is larger in slow metabolizers.
Is there a way to know whether I'm a fast or slow metabolizer without genetic testing?

Self-report tools exist but are imprecise. Some clinicians use a caffeine challenge — track how long it takes for stimulant effects to subside after a measured dose — but this is a rough heuristic. Direct CYP1A2 genotyping is the only reliable method.
